python.explanation:
The algorithm implements the classic bottom-up agglomerative clustering approach. We begin by treating each data point as its own singleton cluster, then repeatedly merge the two closest clusters until we reach the desired cluster count. All pairwise point distances are precomputed upfront to avoid redundant calculations during the merge loop.

Three linkage methods control how inter-cluster distance is defined. Single linkage uses the minimum point-to-point distance between clusters (tends to produce elongated 'chaining' clusters). Complete linkage uses the maximum (tends to produce compact, roughly equal-sized clusters). Average linkage averages all pairwise distances between the two clusters, offering a balance between the two extremes.

Tie-breaking follows the specification: when two cluster pairs share the same distance, we prefer the pair whose first cluster index is smallest, then whose second cluster index is smallest. Since we iterate over sorted cluster IDs with i < j, the (ci, cj) tuple comparison naturally implements this rule. Merges always absorb the larger-indexed cluster into the smaller-indexed one, preserving the invariant that cluster IDs are stable identifiers across iterations.

Finally, labels are assigned by sorting the surviving cluster IDs and mapping them to 0-based integers in order. This ensures deterministic, index-ordered label assignment regardless of which clusters survived the merge sequence.
key_learnings: Agglomerative clustering is O(n³) naively — precomputing all O(n²) pairwise distances once and reusing them is the first optimization to mention in interviews.

Linkage method choice fundamentally changes cluster shape: single linkage is susceptible to chaining (good for elongated shapes), complete linkage favors compact spherical clusters, average is a robust middle ground.
Unlike k-means, agglomerative clustering is deterministic and requires no random initialization — it always produces the same result for the same input and linkage method.
The merge-into-smaller-index rule is a bookkeeping convention; in production implementations (scipy), cluster IDs are tracked in a dendrogram tree structure instead of mutable dicts.

Tie-breaking via lexicographic tuple comparison `(ci, cj) < best_pair` is an elegant pattern — sorting cluster IDs before iteration guarantees ci < cj, making the first element of the tuple the primary sort key automatically.

numpy.explanation:
The NumPy solution leverages vectorized broadcasting to compute the full n×n pairwise distance matrix in a single expression: subtracting the (n,1,f) and (1,n,f) broadcast shapes yields an (n,n,f) difference tensor, then squaring, summing over features, and taking the square root produces the complete distance matrix. This replaces the O(n²·f) Python loop with a single C-level NumPy operation.

A key optimization is maintaining a distance cache (`dist_cache`) between cluster pairs so that inter-cluster distances are only computed when needed and invalidated after merges. When cluster `cj` is absorbed into `ci`, all cache entries involving `cj` are removed, and distances from the now-enlarged `ci` to all remaining clusters are recomputed. This avoids recomputing every pair from scratch each iteration.

For each cluster pair, `np.ix_` constructs an open mesh index that selects the rectangular submatrix of the precomputed point distance matrix corresponding to the two clusters' member points. Then `.min()`, `.max()`, or `.mean()` is applied to that submatrix depending on the linkage method — all vectorized NumPy reductions with no Python-level loops over individual points.

Final label assignment uses NumPy fancy indexing (`labels[clusters[cluster_id]] = label`) to set all cluster member labels in one vectorized write rather than a Python for loop, and the result is converted back to a plain Python list via `.tolist()`.
key_learnings: Broadcasting trick `X[:, np.newaxis, :] - X[np.newaxis, :, :]` is the canonical NumPy idiom for computing all pairwise differences — memorize this pattern for distance-heavy algorithms.
`np.ix_` enables clean rectangular submatrix extraction for fancy indexing across two independent index sets — essential when computing block distances in clustering or attention-like operations.

Caching and invalidating computed values after merges is the key optimization in hierarchical clustering — the Lance-Williams update formula generalizes this to O(n²) total work by updating distances algebraically without recomputing from scratch.

For production use, `scipy.spatial.distance.cdist` computes pairwise distances even more efficiently with optional parallelism and specialized distance metrics — know when to defer to battle-tested library implementations.

The full algorithm is O(n³) time and O(n²) space; the precomputed distance matrix trades memory for speed, which is the right tradeoff for n up to ~10k but breaks down for large datasets where approximate methods (BIRCH, HDBSCAN) are preferred.
